notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s a practical way to think about business deductions and audit risk over the course of the year. This is general education, not tax advice. For your specific situation, it’s worth talking with a CPA or enrolled agent.

1. Big picture: what the IRS actually cares about

For any deduction, the IRS is basically checking three things:

Ordinary – common and accepted in your line of work
Necessary – helpful and appropriate for your business (doesn’t have to be essential)
Substantiated – you can prove:
what you bought
when
how much
business purpose

Higher audit risk: deductions that are big, look personal, or aren’t well documented.

2. Common deductions, risk level, and what to keep each month
A. Home office

Legitimate when:

You have a specific area used regularly and exclusively for business (not the kitchen table).
It’s your principal place of business or where you meet clients.

Audit risk: higher if:

The space isn’t clearly separate or exclusive.
You claim a very large percentage of your home.
Your income is low but the home office deduction is large.

Monthly records to keep:

Rent or mortgage statements
Utilities (electric, gas, water, trash)
Internet bill
Homeowners/renters insurance
Property tax statements (if homeowner)
Simple floor plan with square footage of office vs total home
Photos of the office space showing business use
B. Mileage / vehicle expenses

You can usually choose standard mileage rate or actual expenses (gas, repairs, insurance, etc.).

Legitimate when:

Driving is for business: client meetings, site visits, bank/post office runs, supply runs, etc.
Commuting from home to a regular office is not deductible.

Audit risk: high if:

You don’t keep a contemporaneous mileage log.
You claim very high business use (e.g., 90–100%) on a personal car.
Mileage is large relative to your revenue.

Monthly records to keep:

Mileage log with:
Date
Start/end odometer or miles driven
From/to
Business purpose
Receipts for gas, repairs, insurance, registration (if using actual expenses)
Lease/loan statements for the vehicle

Apps that track mileage automatically can help, but still note the business purpose.

C. Supplies and small tools

Legitimate when:

Items are used in your business: printer ink, paper, packaging, small tools, etc.

Audit risk: low–moderate, unless:

Purchases look personal (e.g., lots of groceries, clothing, home decor).
You mix personal and business on the same card without notes.

Monthly records to keep:

Receipts/invoices (digital copies are fine)
Bank/credit card statements
Simple notes on receipts if the business purpose isn’t obvious (e.g., “client welcome gift”)
D. Software and subscriptions

Legitimate when:

Directly used for business: accounting software, design tools, CRM, cloud storage, email marketing, etc.

Audit risk: low, unless:

It’s clearly personal (e.g., streaming services) without a strong business case.
You try to deduct 100% of mixed‑use items without allocation.

Monthly records to keep:

Invoices/receipts
Subscription confirmations
Notes on what each tool is used for in your business
E. Travel (airfare, hotels, transportation)

Legitimate when:

Primary purpose is business: conferences, client visits, site inspections, etc.

Audit risk: high if:

Trips look like vacations (weekends at resorts, family trips).
You mix personal and business heavily and deduct everything.
You don’t have a clear business agenda or documentation.

Monthly records to keep:

Receipts for airfare, hotels, taxis/Uber, rental cars, parking, tolls
Conference registrations, event agendas
Calendar entries or emails showing meetings/appointments
Notes separating business days vs personal days on each trip
F. Meals

Legitimate when:

Directly related to business: meeting with clients, prospects, or business partners.
Not lavish or extravagant.

Audit risk: high if:

You deduct lots of meals with vague descriptions (“lunch”).
You treat normal personal meals as business.
You don’t record who you met and why.

Monthly records to keep:

Itemized receipts (not just credit card slips)
On each receipt, write:
Who was there
Business relationship
Business purpose (e.g., “Discussed Q3 project scope”)
G. Phone and internet

Legitimate when:

Used for business calls, email, marketing, client communication, etc.

Audit risk: moderate if:

You deduct 100% of a clearly personal family plan.
You can’t explain how you calculated the business percentage.

Monthly records to keep:

Phone and internet bills
A reasonable estimate of business vs personal use (e.g., % of calls, time, or data)
Notes on how you arrived at your business‑use percentage
H. Equipment (computers, cameras, machinery, furniture)

Legitimate when:

Used in your business (laptop, camera, tools, office furniture, etc.).

Audit risk: moderate–high if:

Items are obviously personal (gaming PC, high‑end TV, etc.) with no clear business use.
You claim 100% business use on items that are clearly mixed.
You use accelerated depreciation/Section 179 on large purchases without documentation.

Monthly records to keep:

Purchase receipts/invoices
Serial numbers and model info
Notes on business use and where the item is kept
If mixed use, your estimated business percentage and how you calculated it
3. Behaviors that really raise audit risk

Across all categories, the IRS is more likely to dig in when:

Deductions are large relative to your income
You show consistent losses year after year (looks like a hobby, not a business)
You have poor or missing documentation
You mix personal and business heavily without clear separation

Big takeaway: strong, simple records lower your stress if you’re ever questioned.

4. A simple monthly routine to stay safe

Each month, you could:

Download statements

Bank and credit card statements for all business accounts.

Categorize transactions

Tag each as: home office, mileage/auto, supplies, software, travel, meals, phone/internet, equipment, etc.

Attach receipts

Scan or photograph receipts and attach them in your bookkeeping system or a cloud folder.

Add brief notes

Especially for meals, travel, and anything that could look personal.

Update logs

Mileage log
Home office square footage and any changes
Equipment list (what you own, when purchased, business use)

Again, this is general educational information, not tax advice. A CPA who knows your business, location, and income can:

Confirm which of these apply to you
Help you choose between mi
